title: Two Sum
description: Given an array of integers `nums` and an integer `target`, return the indices of two numbers that add up to the target. You may assume each input has exactly one solution, and you may not use the same element twice.
example:
Input: nums = [2, 7, 11, 15], target = 9
Output: [0, 1]
Explanation: nums[0] + nums[1] = 2 + 7 = 9
hint: Use a hash map to store each number and its index. For every element, check if (target - element) already exists in the map.

title: Reverse a String
description: Write a function that reverses a string in-place. The input is given as an array of characters. Do not allocate extra space for another array — modify it with O(1) extra memory.
hint: Use two pointers: one at the start, one at the end. Swap and move them toward the center.

title: FizzBuzz
description: Print numbers 1 to n. For multiples of 3 print 'Fizz', for multiples of 5 print 'Buzz', and for multiples of both 3 and 5 print 'FizzBuzz'.
example:
Input: n = 15
Output: 1, 2, Fizz, 4, Buzz, Fizz, 7, 8, Fizz, Buzz, 11, Fizz, 13, 14, FizzBuzz
hint: Check divisibility by 15 first, then by 3, then by 5, otherwise print the number.

title: Valid Palindrome
description: Given a string s, return true if it is a palindrome after converting all uppercase letters to lowercase and removing all non-alphanumeric characters.
example:
Input: 'A man, a plan, a canal: Panama'
Output: true
hint: Filter out non-alphanumeric characters, convert to lowercase, then compare the string to its reverse.

title: Maximum Subarray
description: Given an integer array nums, find the subarray with the largest sum, and return its sum.
example:
Input: [-2,1,-3,4,-1,2,1,-5,4]
Output: 6
Explanation: Subarray [4,-1,2,1] has the largest sum = 6
hint: Use Kadane's algorithm: track the current sum and reset to 0 when it goes negative.

title: Merge Two Sorted Arrays
description: Merge two sorted integer arrays nums1 and nums2 into nums1 in-place. nums1 has length m+n where the last n elements are 0 placeholders.
hint: Start from the end of both arrays and fill from the back of nums1. This avoids overwriting unread elements.

title: Binary Search
description: Given a sorted array of integers and a target value, return the index of the target if found, otherwise return -1. You must write an algorithm with O(log n) runtime complexity.
hint: Maintain left and right pointers. Each iteration, check the midpoint and discard half the search space.

title: Linked List Cycle Detection
description: Given the head of a linked list, determine if it contains a cycle. A cycle exists if a node can be reached again by following the next pointer.
example:
Input: 3 → 2 → 0 → -4 (tail connects to node at index 1)
Output: true
hint: Use Floyd's tortoise-and-hare algorithm. Move one pointer one step and the other two steps. If they meet, there's a cycle.

title: Balanced Parentheses
description: Given a string s containing only '(', ')', '{', '}', '[' and ']', determine if the input string is valid. Open brackets must be closed in the correct order.
example:
Input: '()[]{}'
Output: true
Input: '([)]'
Output: false
hint: Use a stack. Push opening brackets; for closing brackets, check if the top of the stack matches.

title: Fibonacci Sequence
description: Calculate the n-th Fibonacci number. F(0) = 0, F(1) = 1, F(n) = F(n-1) + F(n-2). Solve it in O(n) time with O(1) space.
hint: Instead of recursion, use two variables to store the last two Fibonacci numbers and iterate forward.

title: Climbing Stairs
description: You are climbing a staircase. It takes n steps to reach the top. Each time you can climb 1 or 2 steps. In how many distinct ways can you climb to the top?
hint: This is essentially the Fibonacci sequence. ways(n) = ways(n-1) + ways(n-2)

title: Find the Duplicate Number
description: Given an array of n+1 integers where each integer is between 1 and n inclusive, prove that at least one duplicate must exist. Find the duplicate without modifying the array and using only O(1) extra space.
hint: Treat the array as a linked list where nums[i] points to the next node. Use Floyd's cycle detection to find the entry point of the cycle.

title: Group Anagrams
description: Given an array of strings, group the anagrams together. You can return the answer in any order.
hint: Sort each word alphabetically to get a canonical key, then group words with the same key.

title: Rotate Image
description: You are given an n×n 2D matrix representing an image. Rotate the image 90 degrees clockwise in-place.
hint: First transpose the matrix (swap matrix[i][j] with matrix[j][i]), then reverse each row.

title: Longest Common Prefix
description: Write a function to find the longest common prefix string among an array of strings. If no common prefix exists, return an empty string.
hint: Sort the array. The common prefix of the first and last strings is the answer, since all strings fall between them lexicographically.

title: LRU Cache
description: Design a data structure that follows the Least Recently Used (LRU) cache constraint. Implement get(key) and put(key, value) with O(1) average time complexity.
example: LRUCache(2); put(1,1); put(2,2); get(1) → 1; put(3,3); get(2) → -1 (evicted)
hint: Combine a HashMap (O(1) lookup) with a doubly linked list (O(1) move-to-front and evict-from-back).

title: Word Break
description: Given a string s and a dictionary of strings wordDict, return true if s can be segmented into a space-separated sequence of one or more dictionary words.
hint: Use DP where dp[i] is true if the substring s[0..i-1] can be formed. For each position i, try all substrings ending at i.

title: Number of Islands
description: Given an m×n 2D binary grid of '1's (land) and '0's (water), return the number of islands. An island is surrounded by water and formed by connecting adjacent land cells horizontally or vertically.
example:
Input: grid with a 4x5 map
Output: 3
hint: Use BFS/DFS. When you find a '1', increment the count and flood-fill all connected '1's with '0' to avoid counting them again.

title: Product of Array Except Self
description: Given an integer array nums, return an array where each element is the product of all other elements. You must solve it in O(n) time without using the division operator.
hint: Build a prefix product array (left-to-right), then multiply by a suffix product (right-to-left) in a second pass.

title: Validate BST
description: Given the root of a binary tree, determine if it is a valid binary search tree. A BST requires that all left subtree values are less than the node, and all right subtree values are greater.
example:
Input: [2,1,3]
Output: true
Input: [5,1,4,null,null,3,6]
Output: false
hint: Pass down min/max bounds. At each node, verify its value is within the valid range, then tighten the bounds for subtrees.

title: Top K Frequent Elements
description: Given an integer array nums and an integer k, return the k most frequent elements. You may return the answer in any order.
hint: Count frequencies with a hash map, then use bucket sort (index = frequency) to find top-k in O(n).

title: Decode Ways
description: A message containing letters A-Z can be encoded as numbers 1-26. Given a string s of digits, return the number of ways to decode it.
hint: Use DP. dp[i] = ways to decode first i chars. Add dp[i-1] if s[i-1] is valid single digit (1-9), and dp[i-2] if s[i-2..i-1] is valid two-digit number (10-26).

title: 3Sum
description: Given an integer array nums, return all triplets [nums[i], nums[j], nums[k]] such that i ≠ j ≠ k and nums[i] + nums[j] + nums[k] = 0. The solution set must not contain duplicate triplets.
hint: Sort the array. Fix one element and use two pointers for the remaining two. Skip duplicates after each match.

title: Trapping Rain Water
description: Given n non-negative integers representing an elevation map where the width of each bar is 1, compute how much water it can trap after raining.
hint: Use two pointers. The water above any bar is min(maxLeft, maxRight) - height[i]. Process from the side with the smaller max first.

title: Sliding Window Maximum
description: Given an integer array nums and a window size k, return the max value in each sliding window as the window moves from left to right.
hint: Use a monotonic deque. Remove elements from the front if they're out of the window, and from the back if they're smaller than the new element.

title: Merge K Sorted Lists
description: Merge k sorted linked lists and return the merged sorted list as a single linked list.
hint: Use divide-and-conquer: merge two lists at a time, halving the number of lists each round until one remains.

title: Course Schedule (Cycle Detection)
description: There are numCourses courses labeled 0 to numCourses-1. Given a list of prerequisites pairs, determine if it's possible to finish all courses (i.e., no circular dependency).
example:
Input: numCourses=2, prerequisites=[[1,0]]
Output: true
Input: prerequisites=[[1,0],[0,1]]
Output: false
hint: Build a directed graph and detect cycles using DFS with three states: unvisited, visiting (in current path), visited.

title: Serialize and Deserialize Binary Tree
description: Design an algorithm to serialize and deserialize a binary tree. There is no restriction on your serialization format — just make sure you can encode and decode it correctly.
hint: Use preorder DFS with a null marker. Serialize by recursively visiting root, left, right. Deserialize by reading tokens one by one in the same order.

title: Coin Change
description: Given an array of coin denominations and a total amount, return the fewest number of coins needed to make up that amount. If it cannot be done, return -1.
example:
Input: coins=[1,5,11], amount=15
Output: 3 (5+5+5 = 15, but 11+1+1+1+1 = 15 too, and 11+4 invalid… wait: 5+5+5=3 coins)
hint: Classic bottom-up DP. dp[0]=0. For each amount a from 1 to target, try each coin: dp[a] = min(dp[a], dp[a-coin]+1).

title: Find Median from Data Stream
description: Design a data structure that supports addNum(num) to insert a number, and findMedian() to return the median of all inserted elements.
hint: Use two heaps: a max-heap for the lower half and a min-heap for the upper half. Keep them balanced so median is at the tops.

title: Flatten Nested List Iterator
description: Given a nested list of integers, implement an iterator to flatten it. Each element is either an integer or a list whose elements may also be integers or lists.
hint: Use a stack of iterators. When an element is a list, push its iterator; when it's an integer, yield it.

title: Design Twitter Feed
description: Design a simplified version of Twitter where users can post tweets, follow/unfollow each other, and see the 10 most recent tweets in their news feed.
hint: Store tweets as (timestamp, tweetId) per user. getNewsFeed merges the tweet lists of all followed users and returns the top 10 by timestamp.

title: Meeting Rooms II
description: Given an array of meeting time intervals, find the minimum number of conference rooms required.
hint: Sort by start time. Use a min-heap of end times. For each meeting, if the earliest-ending room is free (heap.top <= start), reuse it; otherwise add a new room.

title: SQL: Find Employees Earning More Than Manager
description: Given an Employee table with columns Id, Name, Salary, ManagerId, write a SQL query to find all employees who earn more than their manager.
example:
Table: Employee(Id, Name, Salary, ManagerId)
Output: Name of employees earning > manager
hint: Self-join the Employee table: join Employee e1 (employee) with Employee e2 (manager) where e1.ManagerId = e2.Id.
solution:
SELECT e1.Name AS Employee
FROM Employee e1
JOIN Employee e2 ON e1.ManagerId = e2.Id
WHERE e1.Salary > e2.Salary;
-- Alternatively with subquery:
-- SELECT Name FROM Employee e
-- WHERE Salary > (SELECT Salary FROM Employee WHERE Id = e.ManagerId)

title: SQL: Second Highest Salary
description: Write a SQL query to get the second-highest distinct salary from an Employee table. If there is no second-highest salary, return null.
hint: Use a subquery to find the MAX salary that is less than the overall MAX. Wrap in SELECT to return null when no row exists.
solution:
SELECT MAX(Salary) AS SecondHighestSalary
FROM Employee
WHERE Salary < (SELECT MAX(Salary) FROM Employee);

-- Or with LIMIT/OFFSET (MySQL):
-- SELECT IFNULL(
--   (SELECT DISTINCT Salary FROM Employee ORDER BY Salary DESC LIMIT 1 OFFSET 1),
--   NULL
-- ) AS SecondHighestSalary;

title: Debounce Function
description: Implement a debounce function: given a function fn and a delay ms, return a debounced version that only calls fn after the user stops invoking it for ms milliseconds.
example:
const debouncedFn = debounce(search, 300);
// Rapid calls only trigger search once, 300ms after the last call
hint: Keep a timer reference. Clear it on each call and set a new one. Only when the timer fires does fn get called.

title: Deep Clone Object
description: Write a function to deep clone a plain JavaScript object (no circular references, no special objects like Date/RegExp). Do not use JSON.parse(JSON.stringify()).
hint: Recursively copy each property. If a value is an object, recurse; if it's a primitive, copy directly.

title: Promise.all Implementation
description: Implement your own version of Promise.all that takes an array of promises and resolves when all resolve, or rejects as soon as any one rejects.
hint: Keep a results array and a counter. Resolve when counter reaches the array length. Reject immediately on any rejection.

title: Flatten Array
description: Write a function that flattens a nested array to any specified depth. Do not use Array.prototype.flat().
hint: Recurse through the array. If an element is an array and depth > 0, spread it into results with depth-1; otherwise push it.

title: Event Emitter
description: Implement a simple EventEmitter class with on(event, listener), off(event, listener), and emit(event, ...args) methods.
hint: Use a Map from event name to an array of listener functions. emit calls all listeners; off filters out the matching one.

title: Implement curry()
description: Write a curry function that transforms a function of multiple arguments into a chain of functions each taking one argument.
hint: Return a function that accumulates arguments. When enough args are collected (>= fn.length), call the original function.

title: Git: Interactive Rebase Workflow
description: You have 5 commits on a feature branch with messy commit messages. Describe the exact commands to squash the last 4 commits into the first, keeping a clean history before merging to main.
example:
Before: A → B → C → D → E (messy commits)
After: A → B' (single clean commit)
hint: Use git rebase -i HEAD~4 to open an interactive rebase for the last 4 commits. Change 'pick' to 'squash' (or 's') for commits 2-4.
solution:
# Step 1: Start interactive rebase for last 4 commits
git rebase -i HEAD~4

# Step 2: In the editor, change:
# pick abc1234 second commit
# pick def5678 third commit
# pick ghi9012 fourth commit
# To:
# pick abc1234 second commit
# squash def5678 third commit
# squash ghi9012 fourth commit

# Step 3: Edit the combined commit message
# Step 4: Force push if already on remote
git push --force-with-lease origin feature-branch

title: Docker: Multi-Stage Build
description: Write a Dockerfile for a Node.js app that uses a multi-stage build: a build stage that compiles TypeScript, and a production stage with only the compiled output and runtime dependencies.
example: Result: slim production image without TypeScript compiler or dev dependencies
hint: Use FROM ... AS build for the first stage. In the second stage, use COPY --from=build to selectively copy only the compiled dist/ folder and run npm install --production.
solution:
# Stage 1: Build
FROM node:20-alpine AS build
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci
COPY . .
RUN npm run build

# Stage 2: Production
FROM node:20-alpine
WORKDIR /app
COPY package*.json ./
RUN npm ci --omit=dev
COPY --from=build /app/dist ./dist
EXPOSE 3000
CMD ["node", "dist/index.js"]
# Result: ~150MB vs ~500MB+ without multi-stage

title: Regex: Email Validation
description: Write a regular expression that validates an email address. It should match standard emails like user@example.com and reject obvious invalids like @no-user.com or missing-tld@domain.
hint: Think in three parts: local part (letters, digits, dots, +, hyphens), @ symbol, domain (letters, digits, hyphens), dot, TLD (2+ letters).

title: TypeScript: Generic Utility Types
description: Implement a type-safe DeepReadonly<T> generic that recursively makes all properties of an object (including nested objects) readonly.
hint: Map over all keys with a mapped type. For each value, if it's an object recursively apply DeepReadonly; otherwise keep the type as-is. Use 'readonly' modifier on each key.

title: React: Custom useDebounce Hook
description: Write a custom React hook useDebounce(value, delay) that returns a debounced version of the value — it only updates after the user stops changing it for `delay` milliseconds.
hint: Use useState to hold the debounced value. In useEffect, set a setTimeout that updates the debounced value. Clear the timeout in the cleanup function.

title: REST API: Rate Limiting Design
description: Design a rate limiter for an API that allows 100 requests per minute per user. Describe the algorithm and data structure you'd use, and handle edge cases like burst traffic.
example: User A makes 100 requests in 10 seconds → 101st request rejected until the window resets
hint: Consider: Fixed window (simple but allows 2x burst at boundary), Sliding window log (accurate but memory-heavy), Token bucket (allows controlled bursts). Which fits best?

title: CSS: Centered Element (5 Ways)
description: List 5 different CSS techniques to horizontally and vertically center a div inside its parent container. Discuss when you'd use each.
example:
Parent: 400×400px, Child: 100×100px
→ Child should appear exactly in the center
hint: Think about: Flexbox, CSS Grid, absolute positioning + transform, margin auto (for block elements), and table-cell display.

title: Implement a Queue Using Two Stacks
description: Implement a FIFO queue using only two stacks. The queue should support enqueue(x), dequeue(), and peek() operations.
example:
enqueue(1); enqueue(2); enqueue(3);
dequeue() → 1; peek() → 2; dequeue() → 2
hint: Use one stack for pushing (inbox) and one for popping (outbox). When outbox is empty, pour all of inbox into outbox.

title: Longest Increasing Subsequence
description: Given an unsorted array of integers, return the length of the longest strictly increasing subsequence (elements don't need to be contiguous).
hint: Patience sorting with binary search: maintain an array 'tails' where tails[i] is the smallest tail element of all increasing subsequences of length i+1.
